metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC #2/7) that explicitly exercises the snapshot-absent path: replay zero events against a state dict lacking `claimed_session` entirely and assert that `state.get('claimed_session')` returns `None` without error, proving the interim key-absence contract is exercised by the test suite.
findings.finding: The 'Interim key-presence contract' subsection in What imposes a behavioral requirement on consumers ('Consumers MUST therefore read via `state.get("claimed_session")`) but no AC verifies that internal call-sites or produced test fixtures honor this contract — leaving the described requirement untested by the story's own proof steps.
findings.location: What → 'Interim key-presence contract (until S3)' subsection; Acceptance Criteria (all eight ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the test file: a test that constructs a state dict without the `claimed_session` key (simulating a pre-feature snapshot) and asserts `state.get('claimed_session') is None` — paired with an AC line: 'A state dict lacking `claimed_session` (pre-feature snapshot) returns `None` via `state.get("claimed_session")` and does not raise. Proof: `pytest tests/unit/test_claimed_session.py -k snapshot_absent -q`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a post-rollback schema-state assertion to the plan: specify that after rolling back this story, (a) already-written events with `data["session"]` are harmlessly ignored by the old reducer, (b) any SNAPSHOT event already carrying `claimed_session` in `compiled_state` will still restore the field via the generic key-restore loop (no data loss, no crash), and (c) the net result is that `claimed_session` may be key-present-from-snapshot or key-absent-from-initial-state, leaving consumers in the same coexistence window already described — confirming rollback is safe and no compensating-forward action is needed.
findings.finding: The plan does not assert the schema state AFTER a rollback of this story. After rollback, any compacted SNAPSHOT event already written with `claimed_session` in its `compiled_state` will still restore the field via the generic `process_snapshot` key-restore loop on the rolled-back binary. The plan states forward-compat (older reducer ignores unknown event data keys) but is silent on what compiled state / snapshots look like — and whether consumers are safe — if the code is rolled back after some tickets have been claimed with session ids and potentially compacted.
findings.location: What section — 'Forward/back-compat' paragraph and 'Interim key-presence contract' paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Append a 'Rollback safety' sub-bullet under 'Forward/back-compat': 'Rolling back this story after some tickets have been claimed: (a) events written with `data["session"]` are ignored by the old reducer (unknown data key); (b) any compacted SNAPSHOT already carrying `claimed_session` in its `compiled_state` restores the key via the generic `process_snapshot` loop — the rolled-back binary sees an extra state key it does not use (harmless, same coexistence contract as the pre-S3 window); (c) no data loss and no crash on rollback. Consumers that read via `state.get("claimed_session")` remain safe throughout.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `claimed_session: {type: [string, null]}` to `claim_result.schema.json` beside `assignee`, or explicitly justify in Scope why claim_result.schema.json update is deferred (naming the story that will do it).
findings.finding: claim_result.schema.json is not updated to enumerate claimed_session, leaving the public schema for `rebar claim --output json` (and the MCP claim_ticket output) without the new field. An agent or consumer reading that schema to understand what a claim returns will not see claimed_session, even though it is written to compiled state and directly reflects the claim episode.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"claimed_session": { "type": ["string", "null"] }` to the `properties` block of `src/rebar/schemas/claim_result.schema.json`, mirroring the ticket_state.schema.json addition.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (and corresponding test marker, e.g. `-k non_claim_edge`) that replays a blocked->in_progress STATUS event on a ticket whose claimed_session was previously set and asserts claimed_session is unchanged.
findings.finding: The processing rule 'a later non-claim edge (e.g. blocked->in_progress) leaves claimed_session untouched' is stated in prose but has no AC proof test. The decision boundary for NOT firing _fold_claimed_session on a non-open->in_progress transition is a correctness-critical protocol rule (it prevents wrongly attributing a blocked->in_progress resume to a new session), yet no AC exercises this path.
findings.location: What section — 'Fold (_processors.py)', last paragraph; Scope — 'unit/integration tests' list; Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC: 'Non-claim edge: a `blocked -> in_progress` STATUS event on a ticket with an existing `claimed_session` leaves `claimed_session` unchanged. Proof: `pytest tests/unit/test_claimed_session.py -k non_claim_edge -q`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a persistent record (CLAUDE.md, ADR, or inline design-doc note referenced from the Scope section) documenting the interim key-presence contract so consumers and the S3 author find it without reading this plan.
findings.finding: The interim key-presence contract ('consumers MUST read via state.get("claimed_session")') is a novel behavioral contract for compiled-state consumers during the coexistence window, but no persistent documentation update (CLAUDE.md, ADR, design doc, or consumer-facing changelog) is called for — leaving future consumers or a snapshot-restore path vulnerable to key-absent surprises after this plan text is archived.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line note to CLAUDE.md (or the relevant architecture doc) under compiled-state contracts: '`claimed_session` may be key-absent on pre-S3 snapshots; always read via `state.get("claimed_session")` until story 199b lands.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `_stamp_session` warrants a standalone helper (2 call-sites, ~3 lines of logic) or whether inlining `session = resolve_session_id(); if session: data["session"] = session` at both sites in `claim_core` / `transition_core` is simpler and equally clear.
findings.finding: The `_stamp_session(status_data)` helper proposed in `txn.py` has exactly 2 intra-module call-sites (claim_core and transition_core). The Rule-of-Three threshold for a non-trivial private helper is >=3; below that, inline code avoids premature abstraction. The logic itself is ~3 lines (resolve; if non-None, set key), so the helper adds indirection without reducing complexity.
findings.suggested_fix: Inline the session-stamp logic directly at both sites inside the `data={…}` construction blocks in `claim_core` and `transition_core`, omitting the `_stamp_session` helper entirely. This keeps the byte-identical-when-absent contract visually local to the event-construction site.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale in the What or Why section explaining WHY the event-sourced stamp-and-fold approach is chosen (e.g., replay-determinism — the session id is captured at write time so it is immutable and survives re-replay without re-reading env vars; consistent with how `assignee` and `last_reopened_at` are handled).
findings.finding: The plan gives detailed advisory-specific justifications (T8/T9/G6) for individual design sub-choices but does not state a compact positive rationale for WHY the event-sourced stamp-and-fold approach (write `session` onto the STATUS event data; fold it in the reducer) was chosen over structurally different alternatives — for example, a read-time lookup that derives `claimed_session` from the session_log at compile time, or storing the session directly on the EDIT(assignee) event. The absence of a positive 'why this fits' rationale is a G6(4) gap.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Why section: e.g., 'Stamping the session id onto the STATUS event at write time (rather than deriving it at read/compile time) ensures replay-determinism: the id is immutable once written, survives compaction without re-reading env vars, and is part of the auditable event log — consistent with how `assignee` and `last_reopened_at` are handled in this codebase.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
